# The daemon's mess-notify integration (notify.go's notifyResolution + notify.go's
# notifyViaMess) shells out to a real `mess send` on stage resolution. Hermetic here:
# a fake `mess` script on PATH records its args instead of talking to the real
# messenger, so this test asserts exactly who breeze tried to notify and with what
# message, with no dependency on mess actually being installed or running.
env BREEZE_DIR=$WORK/state
env PATH=$WORK/bin:$PATH
chmod 0755 bin/mess

exec breeze identity register admin
cp stdout admin.token
exec breeze identity register alice
exec breeze identity register ci
exec breeze role assign reviewer alice --as admin --token-file admin.token

exec breeze apply -f pipeline.hcl --as admin --token-file admin.token

# ci triggers build; the next stage (review) requires "reviewer" -> alice should be
# notified, but NOT ci (the actor notifying itself about its own action).
exec breeze stage start release build abc123 --as ci

# The notify callback fires asynchronously (fire-and-forget, no RPC signal) — give it
# a moment before checking what the fake mess script recorded.
exec sleep 0.5
exists mess-calls.log
grep '^send alice breeze: release/build \(abc123\) -> succeeded$' mess-calls.log
! grep '^send ci ' mess-calls.log

exec breeze stop

-- bin/mess --
#!/bin/sh
echo "$@" >> mess-calls.log

-- pipeline.hcl --
pipeline "release" {
  stage "build" {
    type    = "command"
    timeout = "10s"
    command = ["/bin/sh", "-c", "true"]
  }
  stage "review" {
    type               = "approval"
    required_approvals = 1
    approver_role      = "reviewer"
  }
}
